text.heading: The Origin of ESP.
text.p: The origin of ESP can be subdivided into important events that has made it emerge.
They are
- The World War II
- Trade and Expertise alongside the Oil Boom in the Middle East
- Developments in Educational Psychology
text.heading: The World War II
text.p: The world war II ushered in one word great advancement in science and technology. It also brought about what we can refer to as an industrial revolution.
Above this, the world war II also introduced a new set of world powers, they are America and Britain.
Therefore because of the emergence of America and Britain, English language became a globalised language of diplomacy and international relations.

In addition, there was a need to exchange ideas in the areas of science and technology amongst nations. This also brought about the need for non-native English speakers to learn English in order to read manuals, interact with fellow scientists and also study in high technological English speaking environs of Britain and America.
ESP therefore became needed to teach the language along the confines of specific needs with a strong strategic disposition to learner and target needs.
text.heading: Trade and Expertise Alongside the Oil Boom in the Middle East
text.p: Also at this point in the early 70’s the middle east were experiencing an oil boom. There was therefore a need to employ experts and also learn from expertise, not only in language but also in their specific operational duties since the equipments were western.
English therefore became a vehicle for science based, technological based knowledge.
Trade and bilateral trade agreements on oil also increased the need to learn English so as to enhance communication on bargaining powers alongside global policy implementation.
text.heading: Development in Edu-Psychology
text.p: Noam Chomsky of (1964) classical and structural behavioural models of learning thereby giving credence to cognitive approaches to learning.

For Chomsky, Humans are thinking beings who have the ability to express themselves. They are only guided by the structural laws of grammar. Chomsky gives credence to meaning unlike structuralism (which gives credence to structure).

After Chomsky, there were several theories propounded that gave credence to the fact that learners have a role to play in education. One of such is the Affective approach which posits that humans are emotional beings, therefore emotional circumstances may warrant their success in the educational process.
ESP therefore adopts the interplay of the affective and the cognitive approaches, alongside others to prepare a suitable and understanding learning process for the learners.
text.heading: Target Needs
text.p: Target needs can be best explained when the major factors that comprise target needs are addressed. They are
(I) Needs
(II) Wants
(III) Lacks.
Needs are the strategic needs of the particular learner(s) that will make it possible for him or her to be proficient in his or her profession.

Wants are located by the ESP teacher when strategic needs have been determined. This is what the learner wants to know. It is one word strongly connected to the learner’s motivation as this might clash with the teacher’s plans. The ESP teacher therefore needs to locate what the learner actually wants to know, though questionaires or through the response clues being given by the learner(s).

Lacks are the essential language skills which the learner lacks, but which is important for his profession. This can be determined by the ESP teacher by given asking questions, can be gotten from questionaires, and also preliminary tests could also be used.
Certain questions are to be asked by the teacher in order to know the target needs of the learner.
I) The Background of the learner
II) The level of expertise of the learner
III) The Motivation of the learner
I) The Background of the learner
This asks whether he or she has a knowledge of general English or not.
II) The Level of Expertise of the learner
This asks whether the personality(ies) is an artisan, an businessman or a professional, as this will determine their level of exposure.
III) The Motivation of the Learner
This asks why the learner has come to learn Is it one word integrative? that is, is it to be part of the speech community or is it Instrumental, is it for promotion, for accreditation, etc
Others are
(IV) Grading Policy – The grading policy or approach to be used by the teacher. How teachers are evaluated is also considered
text.heading: Learning Needs
text.p: learning Needs is a perspective of the learner on ESP. It is a learner centred approach unlike target needs which is contextual.
Learning Needs asks questions such as
(I) Location – the location of ESP teaching
(II) Duration – The Duration of ESP teaching

p.deleted: (III) Grading Policy – The grading policy that will be used by the

text.p: (III) Motivation – The motivation of the learner to learn. This can be either integrative or instrumental.
(IV)
text.heading: Learning Centred Approach
text.p: This concerns given cognisance to the fact that learners play a role learning and its method play a pivotal role in the lives of learners.
The ESP teacher must therefore look for suitable approaches for learning.
Learning Centred Approaches are as follows

p.deleted: Behaviourism Mentalism Cognitive Approach Cognitive-Affective Approach

text.p: - Natural Approach
This gives credence to the fact that learners are naturally intelligent, they only need guidance to achieve success in learning.
Natural Approach is divided into
- PPPA – Presentation, Practice and Production

one character This gives credence to group discussions, group assignments. This is because the process is aimed at making the learner an avid self-taught proficient individual. The teacher guides the learner but when evaluated allows him to either consult him or not.
There is the use of pictures, images to present and enjoyable learning approach

- Structural Approach Behavioural Approach – This is a learner centred approach that gives credence to rote learning, mimicry and copying. This method is used in teaching foreign or packaged languages in Brazil.
It is considered a cheap, easier learner centred approach as the learner is only made to reproduce what he or she had been taught, without any form of changes.
The teacher uses repetition, text repetition and example repetition to teach.
